---
title: "Narrowfruit Horned Beaksedge (Rhynchospora inundata)"
slug: "rhynchospora-inundata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180793088/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Narrowfruit Horned Beaksedge"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Cyperaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Cyperaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180793088/medium.jpg" alt="Narrowfruit Horned Beaksedge" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/betseyboughton">
betseyboughton</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107488673">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Cyperaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=167877)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats such as bogs, wet flatwoods, and savannas.

## Natural history notes

A grass-like wetland plant characterized by its slender stems and distinctive spikelets with elongated, horn-like projections on the fruits. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as marshes, wet flatwoods, and pond margins, often in sandy soils. It grows during the warmer months and contributes to vegetation structure in wetland communities.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/167877-Rhynchospora-inundata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/genus/1052)
